definition: During parental behavior animals may exhibit nurturing and protective gestures such as birds covering their nestlings with their wings or feeding them.
When feeding animals may show a focused posture such as picking up food with their mouths or paws or bending down to eat attentively.
When animals urinate they may lift their tails arch their bodies or lift a leg during the process.
While resting animals may find a comfortable spot curl up their bodies close their eyes or lazily lie down on the ground.
During circumanal gland signing animals may rub the ground with their hind legs to release specific scent marks for territory or communication.
During play animals may jump run chase or play-fight displaying lively and excited gestures.
When urine signing animals may leave scent marks on the ground or other objects using urine to indicate territory or communicate information.
While defecating animals may squat down raise their tails and defecate on the ground or in a specific area.
During grooming animals may clean their fur or feathers using their tongues or paws to maintain cleanliness and neatness.
While walking animals may move their limbs alternately maintaining balance and moving in a specific direction.
During exploration animals may touch sniff or explore the surrounding environment with their noses mouths or paws to understand new things.
When animals aggregate they may move collectively forming groups or queues to protect or seek resources.
When drinking water animals may lick water with their mouths or tongues or bend down to drink with their mouths.
While smelling animals may press their noses close to the ground or specific objects inhaling deeply to understand the surrounding environment or find food.
While trotting animals may move their limbs quickly but not as fast as running maintaining a relaxed rhythm.
While climbing animals may grip surfaces with their claws or feet stretching and contracting their bodies to climb trees or other structures.
When animals are amused they may sway their bodies wag their tails or make cheerful sounds displaying a happy and excited posture.
During mating animals may take specific postures such as males jumping onto females' backs.
When jumping animals may push off with their hind legs lifting their entire bodies off the ground to move quickly or overcome obstacles.
While foraging animals may search and gather food with their mouths or paws such as looking for plants in grasslands or hunting insects on the ground.
